macro_segments.title: The Mishnah on a Lost Get: Immediate vs. Delayed Recovery

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah: Hamavi Get Ve-Avda Heimenu
Recovery conditions: Immediate recovery is valid; delayed recovery is invalid
Exception: Recovery with recognition markers (purse, wallet, physical identifiers)

macro_segments.title: Reconciling Our Mishnah with Bava Metzia: Documents That Cannot Be Returned

micro_segments.title: Gemara's challenge: documents found cannot be returned to either party
Exception when the original writer says to give it: removes doubt about delivery
Apparent contradiction: Bava Metzia concerns only ownership, not document identity

macro_segments.title: Rava's Resolution: High Traffic Areas vs. Non-Traffic Areas

micro_segments.title: Rava's explanation: concern about different documents only in high-traffic areas
Further limitation: high traffic alone insufficient; requires known duplicate couples
Rava's case of Get found at place of flax: lenient ruling despite high traffic

macro_segments.title: Rav Zeira's Alternative: Contrasting Mishnah with Baraita

micro_segments.title: Rav Zeira compares our Mishnah with a Baraita in Bava Metzia on the same issue
Baraita's explicit ruling: return the Get when the husband agrees, even after long delay
Why Rav Zeira chose Baraita: implicit language in Mishnah leaves ambiguity

macro_segments.title: Stricter Approaches: Rabbi Yirmiya and Rav Ashi on Document Identity Concerns

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yirmiya: return is only valid if witnesses testify they never signed another such Get
Gemara's objection: witnesses themselves could have the same names by coincidence
Rav Ashi: return valid only with precise siman muvhak (absolute identifier mark)
Distinction between siman muvhak (absolute) and regular siman (insufficient for Get)

macro_segments.title: Case of Rabbah Bar Bar Channah: Siman vs. Tvi'at Eina for Document Return

micro_segments.title: Rabbah Bar Bar Channah lost a Get and offered either siman or tvi'at eina (personal recognition)
Uncertainty: unclear whether Get was returned based on siman or tvi'at eina
Legal implication: tvi'at eina restricted to Torah scholars; siman validity depends on Torah/Rabbinic status

macro_segments.title: Measures of Non-Immediacy: How Long Can You Wait and Still Identify a Get?

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Natan: not immediate is duration for a caravan to pass and tarry
Rabbi Shimon ben Eleazar: someone must stand and witness no one passed
Other views: tarrying (not just passing) marks non-immediacy
Rabbi's view: time to write a Get; Rabbi Yitzchak: time to read it

macro_segments.title: Simanim (Identifying Marks) and Physical Evidence for Document Recovery

micro_segments.title: If Get has simanim (identifying marks), they testify in its favor even after long delay
Example of siman muvhak: hole beside a particular letter
Physical characteristics of document (size, parchment color) are insufficient simanim

macro_segments.title: Practical Rule on Non-Immediacy and Its Accepted Standard

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda in name of Shmuel: not immediate when someone actually tarries
Rabbah bar bar Chana in name of Rav Yitzchak: stricter—no one may even pass by

macro_segments.title: Concluding Definitions: Chafisa (Flask) and Deluskama (Elderly Person's Purse)

micro_segments.title: Chafisa explained as a small flask
Deluskama explained as a purse used by elderly people
